name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَةَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ أَنجَىٰكُم مِّنْ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ يَسُومُونَكُمْ سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ وَيُذَبِّحُونَ أَبْنَآءَكُمْ وَيَسْتَحْيُونَ نِسَآءَكُمْ ۚ وَفِى ذَٰلِكُم بَلَآءٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَظِيمٌ (٦)
```

```english
When Moses said to his people, ‘Remember Allah’s blessing upon you when He delivered you from Pharaoh’s clan who inflicted a terrible torment on you, and slaughtered your sons and spared your women, and in that there was a great test from your Lord. (6)
```

```arabic
وَإِذْ تَأَذَّنَ رَبُّكُمْ لَئِن شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ ۖ وَلَئِن كَفَرْتُمْ إِنَّ عَذَابِى لَشَدِيدٌ (٧)
```

```english
And when your Lord proclaimed, “If you are grateful, I will surely enhance you [in blessing], but if you are ungrateful, My punishment is indeed severe.” ’ (7)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰٓ إِن تَكْفُرُوٓا۟ أَنتُمْ وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَغَنِىٌّ حَمِيدٌ (٨)
```

```english
And Moses said, ‘Should you be faithless,—you and everyone on the earth, all together—indeed Allah is all-sufficient, all-laudable.’ (8)
```

```arabic
أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَبَؤُا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَعَادٍ وَثَمُودَ ۛ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ ۛ لَا يَعْلَمُهُمْ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ ۚ جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَرَدُّوٓا۟ أَيْدِيَهُمْ فِىٓ أَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا كَفَرْنَا بِمَآ أُرْسِلْتُم بِهِۦ وَإِنَّا لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّمَّا تَدْعُونَنَآ إِلَيْهِ مُرِيبٍ (٩)
```

```english
Has there not come to you the account of those who were before you—the people of Noah, ‘Ad and Thamud, and those who were after them, whom no one knows [well] except Allah? Their apostles brought them manifest proofs, but they did not respond to them, and said, ‘We disbelieve in what you have been sent with. Indeed we have grave doubts concerning that to which you invite us.’ (9)
```

```arabic
۞ قَالَتْ رُسُلُهُمْ أَفِى ٱللَّهِ شَكٌّ فَاطِرِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ يَدْعُوكُمْ لِيَغْفِرَ لَكُم مِّن ذُنُوبِكُمْ وَيُؤَخِّرَكُمْ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۚ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُنَا تُرِيدُونَ أَن تَصُدُّونَا عَمَّا كَانَ يَعْبُدُ ءَابَآؤُنَا فَأْتُونَا بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ (١٠)
```

```english
Their apostles said, ‘Is there any doubt about Allah, the originator of the heavens and the earth?! He calls you to forgive you a part of your sins, and grants you respite until a specified time.’ They said, ‘You are nothing but humans like us who desire to bar us from what our fathers used to worship. So bring us a manifest authority.’ (10)
```

```arabic
قَالَتْ لَهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ إِن نَّحْنُ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَمُنُّ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ ۖ وَمَا كَانَ لَنَآ أَن نَّأْتِيَكُم بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (١١)
```

```english
Their apostles said to them, ‘Indeed we are just human beings like yourselves; but Allah favours whomever of His servants that He wishes. We may not bring you an authority except by Allah’s leave, and in Allah let all the faithful put their trust. (11)
```

```arabic
وَمَا لَنَآ أَلَّا نَتَوَكَّلَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ وَقَدْ هَدَىٰنَا سُبُلَنَا ۚ وَلَنَصْبِرَنَّ عَلَىٰ مَآ ءَاذَيْتُمُونَا ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ (١٢)
```

```english
And why should we not put our trust in Allah, seeing that He has guided us in our ways? Surely, we will put up patiently with whatever torment you may inflict upon us, and in Allah let all the trusting put their trust.’ (12)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِرُسُلِهِمْ لَنُخْرِجَنَّكُم مِّنْ أَرْضِنَآ أَوْ لَتَعُودُنَّ فِى مِلَّتِنَا ۖ فَأَوْحَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِمْ رَبُّهُمْ لَنُهْلِكَنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٣)
```

```english
But the faithless said to their apostles, ‘Surely we will expel you from our land, or you shall revert to our creed.’ Thereat their Lord revealed to them: ‘We will surely destroy the wrongdoers, (13)
```

```arabic
وَلَنُسْكِنَنَّكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَنْ خَافَ مَقَامِى وَخَافَ وَعِيدِ (١٤)
```

```english
and surely We will settle you in the land after them. This [promise] is for someone who is awed to stand before Me and fears My threat.’ (14)
```

```arabic
وَٱسْتَفْتَحُوا۟ وَخَابَ كُلُّ جَبَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ (١٥)
```

```english
They prayed for victory [against the infidels], and every obstinate tyrant was defeated, (15)
```

```arabic
مِّن وَرَآئِهِۦ جَهَنَّمُ وَيُسْقَىٰ مِن مَّآءٍ صَدِيدٍ (١٦)
```

```english
with hell lying ahead of him, [where] he shall be given to drink of a purulent fluid, (16)
```

```arabic
يَتَجَرَّعُهُۥ وَلَا يَكَادُ يُسِيغُهُۥ وَيَأْتِيهِ ٱلْمَوْتُ مِن كُلِّ مَكَانٍ وَمَا هُوَ بِمَيِّتٍ ۖ وَمِن وَرَآئِهِۦ عَذَابٌ غَلِيظٌ (١٧)
```

```english
gulping it down, but hardly swallowing it: death will assail him from every side, but he will not die, and there is [yet] a harsh punishment ahead of him. (17)
```

```arabic
مَّثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِرَبِّهِمْ ۖ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ كَرَمَادٍ ٱشْتَدَّتْ بِهِ ٱلرِّيحُ فِى يَوْمٍ عَاصِفٍ ۖ لَّا يَقْدِرُونَ مِمَّا كَسَبُوا۟ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلضَّلَـٰلُ ٱلْبَعِيدُ (١٨)
```

```english
A parable of those who defy their Lord: their deeds are like ashes over which the wind blows hard on a tempestuous day: they have no power over anything they have earned. That is extreme error. (18)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses comprise a mention of a portion of Allah’s favors and His punishments during His Days (أيام الله). The apparent context of the verses suggests they are from the speech of Moses (عليه‌السلام), except for the saying of the Exalted: **وَإِذْ تَأَذَّنَ رَبُّكُمْ** (And when your Lord proclaimed...). Thus, it is a narration of the speech of Moses, in which he reminds his people of some of the Days of Allah, the Glorified, as necessitated by His absolute might (العزة المطلقة) in bestowing favors and sending punishments, and placing each in its appropriate position according to His profound wisdom (الحكمة البالغة).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَإِذْ قالَ مُوسى لِقَوْمِهِ اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَةَ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ** — **And when Moses said to his people, ‘Remember Allah’s blessing upon you...** to the end of the verse. The term *sawm* (السوم), as mentioned by al-Raghib, means going in pursuit of something; it is a word for a meaning composed of "going" and "seeking." It is as if in the verse it carries the meaning of "making them taste" the punishment. *Istihya’* (الاستحياء) means the preservation of life.

The meaning is: And remember, O Messenger—to further establish that Allah is All-Mighty, All-Praised—when Moses said to his people, who were the Children of Israel: Remember Allah’s favor upon you on the day He delivered you from the clan of Pharaoh, and specifically from the Copts, while they were persisting in making you taste the worst of punishments, frequently slaughtering the males of your children and sparing the lives of your women for enslavement; and in that was a great trial and tribulation from your Lord.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَإِذْ تَأَذَّنَ رَبُّكُمْ لَئِنْ شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ وَلَئِنْ كَفَرْتُمْ إِنَّ عَذابِي لَشَدِيدٌ** — **And when your Lord proclaimed, “If you are grateful, I will surely enhance you [in blessing], but if you are ungrateful, My punishment is indeed severe.”** It is stated in *al-Majma‘* that *ta’adhdhun* (التأذن) means proclamation (الإعلام); it is said *adhana* and *ta’adhdhana*, similar to *aw‘ada* and *tawa‘‘ada*. End quote.

His saying: **وَإِذْ تَأَذَّنَ رَبُّكُمْ** (And when your Lord proclaimed...) is coordinated (معطوف) with His saying: **وَإِذْ قالَ مُوسى لِقَوْمِهِ** (And when Moses said to his people...). The position of the following verse: **وَقالَ مُوسى** (And Moses said...) in relation to this verse is like the position of His saying: **وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا مُوسى** (Certainly We sent Moses...) in relation to His saying: **كِتابٌ أَنْزَلْناهُ إِلَيْكَ لِتُخْرِجَ النَّاسَ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ** (A Book We have sent down to you that you may bring mankind out from darkness into light...). Understand this, for it is most appropriate to the context of the speech of the Exalted.

Some have mentioned that it is included within the speech of Moses and is not a new sentence (كلام مبتدأ). Based on this, it would be coordinated with His saying: **نِعْمَةَ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ** (Allah’s blessing upon you), and the implied meaning would be: "Remember Allah’s favor upon you and remember when your Lord proclaimed..." However, if that were the case, it would have been more appropriate to say: "Remember when He delivered you and favored you, and when your Lord proclaimed..." to observe the order of events.

It has also been said that it is coordinated with His saying: **إِذْ أَنْجاكُمْ** (when He delivered you), meaning: "Remember Allah’s favor upon you when your Lord proclaimed," for this proclamation itself is a favor due to the encouragement and warning it contains, which motivate one to attain the good of this world and the Hereafter.

The objection to this is that this proclamation is only a favor for the grateful among them specifically; as for others, it is a punishment and a loss for them. Thus, including it in the sequence of what preceded it without restriction or exception is not as it should be.

Therefore, it is apparent that it is a new sentence. The Exalted has explained this reality—namely, that gratitude (الشكر) — *the essence of which is using a blessing in a way that remembers the Giver’s bestowal and manifests His benevolence, and which, in the case of the Exalted, leads to faith in Him and godwariness (التقوى)* — necessitates an increase in blessing, while ingratitude (الكفر) necessitates severe punishment, in several places in His speech. He has narrated from Noah regarding his intimate supplication to his Lord and his call to his people: **فَقُلْتُ اسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ إِنَّهُ كانَ غَفَّاراً يُرْسِلِ السَّماءَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِدْراراً وَيُمْدِدْكُمْ بِأَمْوالٍ وَبَنِينَ** — **telling [them]: ‘Plead to your Lord for forgiveness, indeed He is all-forgiver. He will send for you abundant rains from the sky, and aid you with wealth and sons...** (71:10-12).

Among the subtleties of His generosity (كرمه) appearing in the verse—as some have noted—is its inclusion of an explicit promise (الوعد) and an implicit allusion in the threat (الوعيد), where He said: **لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ** (I will surely enhance you) but said **إِنَّ عَذابِي لَشَدِيدٌ** (My punishment is indeed severe) and did not say "I will surely punish you." This is the habit of the generous in their promises and threats generally.

The verse is absolute (مطلقة); there is no evidence to restrict the promise and threat therein to this world or the Hereafter. The influence of faith (الإيمان), ingratitude (الكفر), godwariness (التقوى), and transgression (الفسق) on the affairs of both the worldly life and the Hereafter is well-known from the Quran.

The verse has been used to argue for the obligation of thanking the Benefactor. The truth is that the verse does not indicate more than that the ingrate is in danger due to his ingratitude, for Allah, the Glorified, did not explicitly state the actuality of punishment for every act of ingratitude when He said: **وَلَئِنْ كَفَرْتُمْ إِنَّ عَذابِي لَشَدِيدٌ** (but if you are ungrateful, My punishment is indeed severe) and did not say: "I will surely punish you."

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَقالَ مُوسى إِنْ تَكْفُرُوا أَنْتُمْ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً فَإِنَّ اللهَ لَغَنِيٌّ حَمِيدٌ** — **And Moses said, ‘Should you be ungrateful—you and whoever is on the earth, all together—indeed Allah is All-sufficient, All-laudable.’** Since the Exalted commanded gratitude for His favors by mentioning what He proclaimed regarding the increase for gratitude and punishment for ingratitude as necessitated by absolute might, He mentioned in support of it from the speech of Moses (عليه‌السلام) that which serves as a parallel. Thus He said: **وَقالَ مُوسى** (And Moses said...), and the discourse continues in this manner for ten verses.

As for Allah being All-sufficient (غني) even if everyone on earth were ungrateful, it is because He is essentially self-sufficient (غني بالذات) from everything; He does not benefit from gratitude nor is He harmed by ingratitude. Rather, the benefit and harm return to the human being in what they perform. As for Him being All-laudable (حميد), it is because praise (الحمد) is the praiser’s manifestation through his tongue of the beauty and goodness of the praised one’s act. The act of the Exalted is good and beautiful from every aspect; it is beautiful with a manifest beauty that is impossible to hide or be hidden. Thus, He, the Exalted, is praised whether a praiser praises Him with the tongue or not.

Furthermore, everything praises Him with the entirety of its existence, even the one who is ungrateful for His blessing, as the Exalted said: **وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ** — **There is not a thing but celebrates His praise** (17:44). Thus, He, the Exalted, is praised whether people praise Him with their tongues or not, and to Him belongs all praise, whether intended for Him or intended for another.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَبَؤُا الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَعادٍ وَثَمُودَ** — **Has there not come to you the account of those who were before you—the people of Noah, ‘Ad and Thamud...** to the end of the verse.

This is from the speech of Moses (عليه‌السلام), reminding his people of the Days of Allah (أيام الله) among the past nations whose individuals have perished, whose breaths have been extinguished, whose traces have been erased, and whose news has been cut off. None knows the reality of their state in detail except Allah, such as the people of Noah, ‘Ad, Thamud, and those after them.

From here, it is known, firstly: that what is meant by the "account" (النبأ) in His saying: **أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَبَؤُا الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ** (Has there not come to you the account of those who were before you) is the news of their destruction and extinction. For an account (النبأ) is news of significant concern; thus, it does not contradict what follows it in His saying: **لا يَعْلَمُهُمْ إِلَّا اللهُ** (none knows them except Allah).

Secondly: that His saying: **قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَعادٍ وَثَمُودَ** (the people of Noah, ‘Ad and Thamud) is by way of mentioning examples, and that His saying: **لا يَعْلَمُهُمْ إِلَّا اللهُ** (none knows them except Allah) is an explanation for His saying: **مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ** (those who were before you). The meaning of the lack of knowledge about them by other than Allah is ignorance of the reality of their state and the lack of encompassing the details of their life history.

It is possible that His saying: **لا يَعْلَمُهُمْ إِلَّا اللهُ** is a parenthetical statement (اعتراض), although what we mentioned is more appropriate to the context. As for the possibility of it being the predicate (خبر) for His saying: **وَالَّذِينَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ** (and those after them), as some have mentioned, its weakness is manifest. Even weaker is the suggestion of some that it is a circumstantial clause (حال) from the pronoun of "those after them," and that His saying: **جاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ** (their apostles came to them) is the predicate for "and those after them."

His saying: **جاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ فَرَدُّوا أَيْدِيَهُمْ فِي أَفْواهِهِمْ** — **Their apostles came to them with manifest proofs, but they put their hands to their mouths...** The apparent meaning is that their messengers came to them with clear arguments that clarify the truth and manifest it without any ambiguity or doubt, yet they prevented them from speaking the truth and blocked their way of speaking.

Thus, the two pronouns in "their hands" (أَيْدِيَهُمْ) and "their mouths" (أَفْواهِهِمْ) refer to the messengers. Putting their hands to their mouths is a metaphor (كناية) for forcing them to be silent and to refrain from speaking the truth, as if they took the hands of their messengers and put them back into their mouths to signal that it is obligatory for you to cease speaking. This is supported by what follows: **وَقالُوا إِنَّا كَفَرْنا بِما أُرْسِلْتُمْ بِهِ وَإِنَّا لَفِي شَكٍّ مِمَّا تَدْعُونَنا إِلَيْهِ مُرِيبٍ** — **and said, ‘We disbelieve in what you have been sent with, and we are indeed in a skeptical doubt regarding what you call us to.’** For the claim of doubt and skepticism in the face of manifest proof and explicit truth—which leaves no room for doubt—cannot occur except from a stubborn denier, an arrogant person, and a reckless tyrant who cannot bear to hear the word of truth and thus forces its speaker into silence.

The commentators have other opinions regarding the meaning of the verse:

Among them is the view of some that the meaning is that the disbelievers put their hands to the mouths of the messengers to belie them and reject what they brought. Thus, the first pronoun refers to the disbelievers and the second to the messengers. The objection to this is that it necessitates a difference in the referents of the two pronouns without a manifest indicator.

Another view: that the disbelievers placed their hands on their own mouths, signaling to the messengers to be silent, as one person does to another when he wants to silence him. Thus, both pronouns refer to the disbelievers.

Another view: that the meaning is they bit their fingers out of intense rage from hearing the call of the messengers. Thus, both pronouns refer to the disbelievers as in the previous view. The objection is that this is a remote metaphor not understood from the wording.

Another view: that "hands" (الأيدي) refers to arguments (الحجج). This is either the plural of *yad* (يد) meaning the limb, because the argument is like the hand with which one strikes and defends; or the plural of *yad* meaning favor (النعمة), because the arguments of the messengers are favors from them upon the people. The meaning would be that they returned the messengers' arguments to their mouths from which they emerged.

Close to this view is the saying of some: that "hands" refers to the favors of the messengers, which are their commands and prohibitions, and both pronouns also refer to the messengers. The meaning is that they belied the messengers in their commands and prohibitions.

Also close to it is another saying: that "hands" refers to favors, and the pronoun in "their hands" refers to the messengers, while the preposition *fi* (في) in His saying "in their mouths" (فِي أَفْواهِهِمْ) carries the meaning of "with" (بـ), and the pronoun refers to the disbelievers. The meaning is that the disbelievers belied with their mouths the favors of the messengers, which are their arguments.

You are well aware that these are meanings remote from understanding; His speech, the Exalted, is too sublime to be interpreted by these and their like.

As for His saying: **وَقالُوا إِنَّا كَفَرْنا بِما أُرْسِلْتُمْ بِهِ وَإِنَّا لَفِي شَكٍّ مِمَّا تَدْعُونَنا إِلَيْهِ مُرِيبٍ** (and said, ‘We disbelieve in what you have been sent with, and we are indeed in a skeptical doubt regarding what you call us to’), it is a kind of explanation for His saying: **فَرَدُّوا أَيْدِيَهُمْ فِي أَفْواهِهِمْ** (but they put their hands to their mouths). The first sentence—namely, their saying: "We disbelieve in what you have been sent with"—is a denial of the divine law (الشريعة الإلهية) which is the core of the message. The second sentence—namely, their saying: "and we are indeed in a skeptical doubt..."—is a denial of the arguments and manifest proofs they brought, and a manifestation of skepticism in what they were calling toward, which is the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قالَتْ رُسُلُهُمْ أَفِي اللهِ شَكٌّ فاطِرِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ يَدْعُوكُمْ لِيَغْفِرَ لَكُمْ مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ وَيُؤَخِّرَكُمْ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى** — **Their apostles said, ‘Is there any doubt about Allah, the Originator of the heavens and the earth?! He calls you to forgive you some of your sins, and to grant you respite until a specified time.’** The root of *fatr* (الفطر), as mentioned by al-Raghib, is splitting lengthwise. It is said: *fatartu al-shay’a fatran*, meaning I split it lengthwise. *Aftara al-shay’u futuran* and *infatara infitaran* mean it accepted the split. It is used in the Quran regarding what is attributed to Him, the Exalted, in the sense of bringing into existence (الإيجاد) with a kind of special consideration, as if He, the Exalted, split non-existence (العدم) and brought things out from its belly. Thus, they are manifest as long as He, the Exalted, holds the two sides of non-existence apart; they exist as long as He is holding them. If He were to cease holding, they would be annihilated and vanish, as the Exalted said: **إِنَّ اللهَ يُمْسِكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ أَنْ تَزُولا وَلَئِنْ زالَتا إِنْ أَمْسَكَهُما مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ** — **Indeed Allah sustains the heavens and the earth lest they should decline; and were they to decline, there is none who can sustain them after Him** (35:41).

Accordingly, the interpretation of *fatr* as "creation" (الخلق) — *which is the gathering of parts and portions* — as occurs in some expressions, is not as it should be. This is supported by the fact that if *fatr* were in the sense of creation, the proof alluded to in His saying: **فاطِرِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** (Originator of the heavens and the earth) would be aimed at proving the existence of the Creator. This would be foreign to the context because the idolaters (الوثنية) do not deny the existence of a Creator for the world, and that He is Allah, exalted is His Name, and none other. Rather, they deny the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) and worship (العبادة) — *which is that Allah, the Glorified, is the only Lord and the only One to be worshipped* — and the proof of Him being the Creator of the heavens and the earth does not benefit in that regard.

In any case, His saying: **قالَتْ رُسُلُهُمْ أَفِي اللهِ شَكٌّ** (Their apostles said, ‘Is there any doubt about Allah...’) is a speech countered by their saying: **وَقالُوا إِنَّا كَفَرْنا بِما أُرْسِلْتُمْ بِهِ وَإِنَّا لَفِي شَكٍّ مِمَّا تَدْعُونَنا إِلَيْهِ مُرِيبٍ** (and said, ‘We disbelieve in what you have been sent with, and we are indeed in a skeptical doubt regarding what you call us to’). You have already learned that this saying of theirs includes two denials: their denial of the message and their skepticism regarding the monotheism of Lordship. Thus, the speech of the messengers, brought as a response to their saying by way of counter-argument, includes two parts.

Their saying: **أَفِي اللهِ شَكٌّ فاطِرِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** (Is there any doubt about Allah, the Originator of the heavens and the earth?!) is a proof for the monotheism of Lordship. For if it were intended merely as a rejection of the disbelievers without an allusion to a proof, there would be no need to mention the attribute "Originator of the heavens and the earth." Its mention indicates that it removes every doubt and skepticism regarding Him, the Exalted.

This is because we see, at the first instance of our intellect, that this witnessed world—which is composed of many things, each of which is limited in itself and distinguished from others in existence—does not have its existence, nor the existence of any of its parts, from itself or subsisting by its own essence. Otherwise, it would not change or be annihilated. Thus, its existence and the existence of its parts, as well as everything that pertains to existence such as attributes and effects, are from another and for another. This "other" is what we call "Allah," exalted is His Name.

He, the Exalted, is the One who brings the world and every part of its parts into existence, limits it, and distinguishes it from others. Thus, He is in Himself existent and unlimited; otherwise, He would need another to limit Him. Therefore, He, the Exalted, is One (واحد) and does not accept multiplicity, because that which is not limited by a boundary does not accept multiplicity.

In His oneness, He manages every affair just as He brings it into existence, because He is the Owner of their existence, and every affair returns to their existence. No other shares with Him in anything because none of the existents other than Him owns anything for itself or for another. Thus, He, the Exalted, is the Lord of everything, there is no lord besides Him, just as He is the Originator of everything, there is no originator besides Him.

This is a complete proof, easily accessible even to simple understandings. It is attained by the human being who acknowledges by his primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — that the witnessed world has a reality and facticity, without being a mere illusion as suggested by sophistry and skepticism. Through it, the oneness of Divinity (الألوهية) and Lordship (الربوبية) is established. For this reason, it was employed in this context, which is the context of disputation with the idolaters.

From here, the error of the claim of those who think that His saying: **أَفِي اللهِ شَكٌّ فاطِرِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** is an argument aimed at proving a Creator for the world becomes apparent. Likewise, the saying of those who say it is a proof of the continuity of management for the monotheism of Lordship. Rather, it is a proof for Him, the Exalted, from the perspective of the subsistence of the existence of everything and the effects of its existence by Him from every aspect. Thus, it results in His oneness in Lordship and removes the skepticism they supported with their saying: **وَإِنَّا لَفِي شَكٍّ مِمَّا تَدْعُونَنا إِلَيْهِ مُرِيبٍ** (and we are indeed in a skeptical doubt regarding what you call us to). (1)

Then their saying: **يَدْعُوكُمْ لِيَغْفِرَ لَكُمْ مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ وَيُؤَخِّرَكُمْ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى** (He calls you to forgive you some of your sins, and to grant you respite until a specified time) is an allusion to the proof of prophethood (النبوة) which they denied with their saying: **إِنَّا كَفَرْنا بِما أُرْسِلْتُمْ بِهِ** (We disbelieve in what you have been sent with), by which they meant the religion of the messengers and the heavenly law through revelation (الوحي).

Its explanation is that it is from His established sunnah (سنة) to guide everything to its perfection and its specific happiness. The human being is one of these species included in the divine guidance. Thus, it is obligatory in the divine providence (العناية الإلهية) that he be guided to the happiness of his life. However, he has an eternal life, not limited by this world nor cut off by death. His happiness in life is to live in this world a tranquil life based on the moderation of his faculties in enjoying the provisions of life—food, drink, clothing, marriage, and so on—which are the righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة); and in the Hereafter, to live based on what he acquired of true belief and righteous deeds.

Although he is equipped with a primordial nature (الفطرة) that reminds him of true belief and righteous deeds, he is, from another side, fashioned for social life, which calls him to follow desires, injustice, and transgression. Thus, the mere reminder of the primordial nature is not sufficient to carry him upon a true and just sunnah that achieves for him steadfastness in belief and action. Otherwise, human society would never have been corrupted, nor any of its parts, while they are equipped with the primordial nature.

Therefore, it is obligatory in the divine providence to aid the human species—along with its primordial nature calling to goodness and happiness—with another matter through which divine guidance is received. This is prophethood (النبوة) — *a pure human station where true belief and righteous deeds are disclosed to him through divine revelation and celestial speech* — the following of which guarantees the happiness of the individual and society in this world and the Hereafter.

As for the happiness of this world, it is because—as has been mentioned repeatedly—there is a necessary connection between sins and injustices and the divine exemplary punishment and penalty that ends in destruction. If society were not corrupted and remained upon the primordial goodness, destruction would not be chosen for them, nor would exemplary punishment surprise them, and they would live for the natural terms (الآجال الطبيعية) decreed for them and a life to be envied.

As for the happiness of the Hereafter, it is because following the divine call—in other words, faith and godwariness—adorns the soul with a righteous form and removes the filth of the soul, which is sins (الذنوب), in proportion to the following.

Thus, His Lordship (الربوبية) over everything, which necessitates managing them with the best management, and His guidance of every species to its happy end, requires that He care for people by sending messengers from among them to them, and His calling people through the tongues of His messengers to faith and righteous deeds, so that their happiness in this world and the Hereafter may be completed. In this world, by escaping the exemplary punishment and penalty that would finish them; and in the Hereafter, through divine forgiveness (المغفرة) in proportion to the faith and righteous deeds they donned.

Once you know what we have mentioned, it becomes clear to you that **His saying, the Exalted**, narrating from the messengers: **يَدْعُوكُمْ لِيَغْفِرَ لَكُمْ مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ وَيُؤَخِّرَكُمْ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى** (He calls you to forgive you some of your sins, and to grant you respite until a specified time) is an allusion from them (peace be upon them) to the argument for general prophethood. His saying: **لِيَغْفِرَ لَكُمْ** (to forgive you...) is an allusion to the goal of the call in the Hereafter, and His saying: **وَيُؤَخِّرَكُمْ** (and to grant you respite...) is an allusion to its goal in this world. He prioritized what pertains to the Hereafter over what pertains to this world because the Hereafter is the primary objective and it is the permanent abode (دار القرار).

They attributed the call in their speech to Allah, the Glorified, to draw attention to what is the truth in contrast to the disbelievers' saying: **تَدْعُونَنا إِلَيْهِ** (what you call us to), where they attributed it to the messengers. His saying: **مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ** (some of your sins) is apparent in partitivity (التبعيض). Perhaps it is to indicate that forgiveness is in proportion to obedience, and human society is never free from sin that necessitates accountability. Thus, what is forgiven in any case is some of the sins of society, not all of them. Understand this.

Perhaps some have mentioned that what is meant is that He forgives the rights of Allah, not the rights of people. This was refuted by the fact that it is authentically reported from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that "Islam cuts off what preceded it."

It has also been said that *min* (مِنْ) is redundant (زائدة), supported by His saying, the Exalted, in another place: **يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ** (He will forgive you your sins) without *min*. The objection to this is that *min* is only added in negation, not in affirmation, such as their saying: "No man came to me," and it enters upon the indefinite, not the definite, as has been said. Furthermore, the context of the two verses is different; for His saying: **يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ**, which is apparent in the forgiveness of all, is in the context of faith and jihad, which is His saying: **تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَتُجاهِدُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ بِأَمْوالِكُمْ وَأَنْفُسِكُمْ — يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ** — **[that] you have faith in Allah and His Apostle and wage jihad in the way of Allah with your possessions and your persons... He will forgive you your sins** (61:11-12). Whereas what Allah narrated from Noah (عليه‌السلام) in a similar context—and he is the first of these messengers mentioned in the verse—is His saying: **أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ وَاتَّقُوهُ وَأَطِيعُونِ يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ وَيُؤَخِّرْكُمْ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى** — **‘Worship Allah and be wary of Him, and obey me, that He may forgive you some of your sins and grant you respite until a specified time’** (71:3-4). This agrees with the verse we are currently discussing; thus, partitivity is apparently unavoidable.

Among what has been said in justifying the verse is that "some" refers to "all" by way of extension (توسع). Also, that what is meant is the forgiveness of sins committed before faith, while what is after that is left unmentioned. Also, that what is meant is the forgiveness of major sins (الكبائر), which are some of the sins, and so on. These are weak views not to be reckoned with.

Al-Zamakhshari said in *al-Kashshaf*: "If you ask: What is the meaning of partitivity in His saying: **مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ**? I say: I have not known it to come like this except in the address to the disbelievers, in His saying: **وَاتَّقُوهُ وَأَطِيعُونِ يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ** and **يا قَوْمَنا أَجِيبُوا داعِيَ اللهِ وَآمِنُوا بِهِ يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ**. Whereas He said in the address to the believers: **هَلْ أَدُلُّكُمْ عَلى تِجارَةٍ تُنْجِيكُمْ مِنْ عَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ — يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ** and others that induction will show you. This was to differentiate between the two addresses, and so as not to equate the two groups in the promise." End quote.

It is as if his meaning is that the sins forgiven in both groups are the same—namely, all sins—except that the honor of the station of faith necessitated explicitly stating the forgiveness of all for the believers, while sufficing in the promise to the disbelievers with the forgiveness of some and remaining silent about the rest. The forgiveness of some does not contradict the forgiveness of the others. Let this be his meaning; otherwise, mere differentiation between the two addresses does not result in committing a total violation of reality.

His saying: **وَيُؤَخِّرَكُمْ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى** (and to grant you respite until a specified time) means He does not hasten the penalty and destruction upon you, and He delays you until the term that is not delayed, which He has specified for you, and the word is not changed with Him. It has already been mentioned in the commentary of the beginning of Surah al-An‘am that the term (الأجل) is of two kinds: a suspended, conditional term (أجل موقوف معلق), and a specified term (أجل مسمى) that is not delayed.

Among the evidence for what we have mentioned is the saying of Noah to his people in this context, according to what Allah, the Glorified, narrated: **وَيُؤَخِّرْكُمْ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى إِنَّ أَجَلَ اللهِ إِذا جاءَ لا يُؤَخَّرُ** — **‘and grant you respite until a specified time. Indeed Allah’s [appointed] time cannot be deferred when it comes’** (71:4).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قالُوا إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُنا تُرِيدُونَ أَنْ تَصُدُّونا عَمَّا كانَ يَعْبُدُ آباؤُنا فَأْتُونا بِسُلْطانٍ مُبِينٍ** — **They said, ‘You are nothing but humans like us who desire to bar us from what our fathers used to worship; so bring us a manifest authority.’** It has already been mentioned in the discussions on prophethood in the second volume of the book that the miraculous sign (الآية المعجزة) is a general proof for the prophethood of the prophet, not a common or specific proof. Revelation and prophethood—which are a kind of connection with the Unseen (الغيب)—are matters that break the habit (خارق للعادة) current among human individuals, which they do not find within themselves. Thus, whoever claims it must provide proof, and there is no way to prove it except by bringing another miracle that breaks the habit, indicating the correctness of this connection with the Unseen. This is because the judgment of similar things is one; if it is possible for the habit to be broken by one thing, it is possible for it to be broken by its like.

When the messengers (peace be upon them) argued against the disbelievers of their nations regarding general prophethood with their saying: **يَدْعُوكُمْ لِيَغْفِرَ لَكُمْ مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ وَيُؤَخِّرَكُمْ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى**, the disbelievers returned to them by demanding proof for what they claimed of prophethood for themselves, excusing themselves in that with their saying: **إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُنا** (You are nothing but humans like us). Then they explicitly stated what they demanded of proof—namely, the miraculous sign—with their saying: **فَأْتُونا بِسُلْطانٍ مُبِينٍ** (so bring us a manifest authority).

The meaning is: We concede that divine providence necessitates calling us to forgiveness and mercy, but we do not concede to you that this call is established through you as you claim, for you are humans like us, not exceeding us in anything. If mere humanity necessitated that, we would have found it within ourselves, and we are humans. So if you are truthful in this claim of yours, then bring us a manifest authority—that is, a decisive proof that dominates our intellects and compels us to acknowledge your prophethood, which is a celestial miraculous sign that breaks the habit just as what you claim is a similar break.

Through this explanation, it becomes clear, firstly: that this speech of theirs is of the nature of denying the claim (منع الدعوى), and their saying: "You are nothing but humans like us" is the basis of the denial (سند المنع), and their saying: "so bring us a manifest authority" is an explicit demand for proof.

Secondly: that their saying: **تُرِيدُونَ أَنْ تَصُدُّونا عَمَّا كانَ يَعْبُدُ آباؤُنا** (who desire to bar us from what our fathers used to worship) is of the nature of an objection occurring between the denial and its basis. Its meaning is: Since you are humans like us, having no merit over us in anything, there is no reason for us to accept from you what we do not find within ourselves nor recognize from those like us. What we recognize from such matters is that they only emerge from worldly, material goals and ambitions; so it is nothing but that you want to turn us away from our national sunnah and our exemplary way.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قالَتْ لَهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ إِنْ نَحْنُ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ وَلكِنَّ اللهَ يَمُنُّ عَلى مَنْ يَشاءُ** — **Their apostles said to them, ‘We are indeed nothing but humans like you, but Allah favors whomever He wishes...’** to the end of the verse. This is the response of the messengers to what they raised against their message—that you are humans like us, so you do not possess a celestial identity such that you could connect with the Unseen; so if you are truthful in this claim of celestial power, then bring us a manifest authority.

The summary of the response is: Our being humans like you is conceded, but it necessitates the opposite of what you concluded. As for your saying that our being humans like you necessitates that we should not be distinguished by a characteristic you do not find within yourselves—namely, revelation and message—the answer is: Similarity in humanity does not necessitate similarity in all formal and spiritual human perfections. Just as the balance of creation and beauty of form, as well as the gravity of intellect and correctness of opinion, understanding, and intelligence, are formal and spiritual perfections found in some human individuals and not others, it is possible for Allah to bestow the favor of revelation and message upon some of His servants and not others, for Allah favors whomever He wishes of them.

As for your saying: "so bring us a manifest authority," it is based on the prophet being a celestial personality and a celestial power effective in whatever he wishes. This is not so; the prophet is nothing but a human like you to whom the message is revealed, and he has no part in the matter. It is not for him to bring a sign from himself unless Allah wishes that and permits it.

Thus, His saying: **إِنْ نَحْنُ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ** (We are indeed nothing but humans like you) is a concession by the messengers to their saying: "You are nothing but humans like us," to conclude the opposite of what they concluded from it. His saying: **وَلكِنَّ اللهَ يَمُنُّ عَلى مَنْ يَشاءُ** (but Allah favors whomever He wishes) is an allusion to a premise (مقدمة) which, when joined, yields the desired conclusion. His saying: **وَما كانَ لَنا أَنْ نَأْتِيَكُمْ بِسُلْطانٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ** (and it is not for us to bring you an authority except by Allah’s leave) is a response they concluded from their being humans like them.

The conclusion of this speech with their saying: **وَعَلَى اللهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ** (and in Allah let all the faithful put their trust) is to allude to what serves as a second argument for returning the entire matter—including the matter of the miraculous sign—to Allah. This is an argument specific to the believers, the summary of which is: Faith in Allah, the Glorified, necessitates that they acknowledge that bringing the sign belongs only to Allah, because power and strength belong to Him specifically; none other owns anything of that except by His leave.

This is because He is Allah, exalted is His station; He is the One from Whom everything begins and to Whom everything ends, and by Whom everything subsists. Thus, He is the Lord of everything, the Owner of the management of its affair; nothing owns an affair except by His leave. Therefore, He is the Trustee (الوكيل) of everything, the One standing by what pertains to it of the affair. So the believer must take his Lord as a trustee in all that pertains to him, even in his actions attributed to him, since all power belongs to Him, the Glorified. And the messenger must acknowledge that he does not have the power to bring a miraculous sign except by Allah’s leave.

The verse is apparent in that the messengers (peace be upon them) did not claim the impossibility of their bringing the miraculous sign called "manifest authority"; rather, they claimed the impossibility of their being independent in that without the need for the leave of Allah, the Glorified, and they argued for that firstly and secondly.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَما لَنا أَلَّا نَتَوَكَّلَ عَلَى اللهِ وَقَدْ هَدانا سُبُلَنا وَلَنَصْبِرَنَّ عَلى ما آذَيْتُمُونا وَعَلَى اللهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ** — **And why should we not put our trust in Allah, seeing that He has guided us in our ways? We will surely endure your persecution of us, and in Allah let all the trusting put their trust.** The "what" (ما) is interrogative, and the interrogation is for denial (الإنكار). His saying: **وَقَدْ هَدانا سُبُلَنا** (seeing that He has guided us in our ways) is a circumstantial clause (حال) from the pronoun in "we" (لَنا). The "ways" (سبل) of the prophets and messengers are the laws (الشرائع) they were calling toward. The Exalted said: **قُلْ هذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُوا إِلَى اللهِ عَلى بَصِيرَةٍ** — **Say, ‘This is my way: I summon to Allah with insight’** (12:108). The meaning is: What excuse do we possess for not putting our trust in Allah while the state is that He, the Exalted, has guided us to our ways, and we had no part in this favor and happiness He bestowed upon us? So if He, the Glorified, has done this act with us, which is all goodness, then it is obligatory that we trust in Him in all other matters.

This is, in reality, a second argument for the obligation of trusting in Him and handing over the reins to Him. It follows the path of the effects indicating the obligation of trusting in Him, just as the previous argument followed the path of looking at the Agent Himself. The report of the argument is that His guidance of us to our ways is a proof for the obligation of trusting in Him, because He does not betray His servants and desires nothing for them but goodness. With the existence of the proof for trust, there is no meaning for the existence of a proof for the lack of trust that would be an excuse for us; thus, we have no way to not trust in Him, the Exalted.

So His saying, the Exalted: **وَعَلَى اللهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ** (and in Allah let all the faithful put their trust) serves as the *limm* (اللم) — *the reasoning from cause to effect* — and His saying: **وَما لَنا أَلَّا نَتَوَكَّلَ عَلَى اللهِ وَقَدْ هَدانا سُبُلَنا** serves as the *inn* (الإن) — *the reasoning from effect to cause*. So reflect on this sweet explanation and the easy yet profound argumentation that the Noble Quran presents to its reflectors in the briefest wording.

His saying: **وَلَنَصْبِرَنَّ عَلى ما آذَيْتُمُونا** (We will surely endure your persecution of us) is a derivation of patience (الصبر) from what was explained of the obligation of trusting in Him. That is: If it is obligatory that we trust in Him while we are believers in Him and He has guided us to our ways, then we shall surely endure your harming of us in the path of calling to Him, trusting in Him until He judges as He wishes and does what He wills, without us seeking refuge in what we possess of apparent power and strength.

His saying: **وَعَلَى اللهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ** (and in Allah let all the trusting put their trust) is a speech based on progression (الترقي). That is: Everyone who dons the quality of trust must trust in Allah, whether he is a believer or not, since there is no proof for other than Him. However, the one who trusts with the reality of trust (حقيقة التوكل) is none but a believer, for he acknowledges that the entire matter belongs to Allah; thus, he cannot but obey Him in what He commands, refrain from what He forbids, be pleased with what He is pleased with, and be displeased with what He is displeased with—and this is faith.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِرُسُلِهِمْ لَنُخْرِجَنَّكُمْ مِنْ أَرْضِنا أَوْ لَتَعُودُنَّ فِي مِلَّتِنا** — **The faithless said to their apostles, ‘We will surely expel you from our land, or you shall return to our creed.’** This is a threat from them after they were incapable in their debate and lost in their argumentation. The address in their saying: "We will surely expel you..." is to the messengers and those who believed with them; for they would not be satisfied that the messengers return to their creed while their followers remain upon the religion of monotheism (التوحيد). Furthermore, Allah, the Glorified, explicitly stated that in the stories of some of them, such as His saying regarding Shu‘ayb: **قالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ لَنُخْرِجَنَّكَ يا شُعَيْبُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَكَ مِنْ قَرْيَتِنا أَوْ لَتَعُودُنَّ فِي مِلَّتِنا** — **The elite of his people who were arrogant said, ‘O Shu‘ayb, we will surely expel you and those who have faith with you from our town, or you shall return to our creed’** (7:88).

His saying: **أَوْ لَتَعُودُنَّ فِي مِلَّتِنا** (or you shall return to our creed). *‘Ada* (عاد) is among the incomplete verbs (الأفعال الناقصة) meaning "to become" (الصيرورة), which is the transition from one state to another, whether one was upon it previously or not. Among the evidence for this—as has been said—is His saying: **فِي مِلَّتِنا** (in our creed). If it were in the sense of returning to what was, it would have been necessary to say: *ila millatina* (to our creed).

From here, the error of what has been said—that the apparent meaning of the verse is that the messengers were upon their creed before the message, so the disbelievers tasked them to return to what they were upon—becomes apparent.

Furthermore, their address was not to the messengers specifically, but to them and to those who believed in them from among those who were upon the creed of the disbelievers before. Thus, the address to them and their messengers to return to their creed—on the assumption that "return" means going back—is by way of predominance (التغليب).

Among the subtleties of the craft in the verse is the entry of the *lam* of the oath (لام القسم) and the *nun* of emphasis (نون التأكيد) on both sides of the alternative: "We will surely expel you or you shall surely return," even though "or" (أو) is for rectification (الاستدراك) and conveys the meaning of exception. There is no meaning in saying: "except that you return, by Allah, to our creed," except that their return, since it was by compulsion from the disbelievers, was in the sense of "re-induction." Thus, His saying: **لَتَعُودُنَّ** became a side of the alternative, and the entry of the *lam* and *nun* became correct, and the meaning became: "By Allah, we will surely expel you from our land or we will surely re-induct you into our creed."

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَأَوْحى إِلَيْهِمْ رَبُّهُمْ لَنُهْلِكَنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ وَلَنُسْكِنَنَّكُمُ الْأَرْضَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ** — **Then their Lord revealed to them: ‘We will surely destroy the wrongdoers, and We will surely settle you in the land after them...’** to the end of the verse. The first and second plural pronouns refer to the messengers, and the third to those who disbelieved, as indicated by the context. Describing them as "the wrongdoers" (الظالمين) is to indicate that their wrongdoing is the cause for destruction; for attaching a judgment to an attribute suggests causality (العلية). Likewise, His saying: **ذلِكَ لِمَنْ خافَ مَقامِي وَخافَ وَعِيدِ** (That is for someone who fears My station and fears My threat) suggests the causality of fear for the settlement.

His saying: **مَقامِي** (My station) is a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) meaning His standing, the Exalted, over the entire affair; or a noun of place (اسم مكان) meaning the rank of His self-subsistence (قيمومته) over the entire affair. What is meant by His threat (وعيده) is what He threatened the opposers of His command with of punishment.

Thus, what is meant by fear of His station, the Exalted, is godwariness (تقواه) of Him as He is Allah, the One standing over the affairs of His servants. And what is meant by fear of His threat is godwariness of Him as He is Allah, who warned His servants against opposing His command through the tongues of His prophets and messengers. In any case, it returns to godwariness (التقوى) and corresponds to the saying of Moses to his people: **اسْتَعِينُوا بِاللهِ وَاصْبِرُوا إِنَّ الْأَرْضَ لِلَّهِ يُورِثُها مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ وَالْعاقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ** — **‘Seek help in Allah and be patient. Indeed the earth belongs to Allah, and He gives its inheritance to whomever He wishes of His servants, and the outcome will be in favour of the God-conscious’** (7:128), as alluded to in *al-Kashshaf*.

The meaning is: Then the Lord of the messengers revealed to them—and the attribute of specific Lordship (الربوبية) was taken for them due to their trust (توكل) which attracts mercy and providence—and He swore: We will surely destroy these who threaten you because of their wrongdoing, and We will surely settle you in this land from which they threatened to expel you, and We will make you inherit it because of your quality of fearing Me and My threat. And thus We do, making Our God-conscious servants inherit the earth.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَاسْتَفْتَحُوا وَخابَ كُلُّ جَبَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ** — **They sought victory, and every obstinate tyrant was disappointed.** *Istiftah* (الاستفتاح) is seeking opening and victory. *Khaybah* (الخيبة) is the cutting off of hope, loss, and destruction. The *‘anid* (العنيد) is the stubborn one, and from it comes the "antagonist" (المعاند).

The pronoun in "they sought victory" (وَاسْتَفْتَحُوا) refers to the messengers; that is, they sought victory from Allah when the means were cut off from them on every side, and the wrongdoing of the wrongdoers and the belying of the antagonists reached its peak, like the saying of Noah in what Allah narrated: **أَنِّي مَغْلُوبٌ فَانْتَصِرْ** — **‘I am overcome, so help [me]!’** (54:10). It is possible for the pronoun to refer to the messengers and the disbelievers all together, for the disbelievers also used to insist that the messengers bring what would judge between them, like their saying: **مَتى هذَا الْفَتْحُ** — **‘When will this victory be...’** (32:28) and **مَتى هذَا الْوَعْدُ** — **‘When will this promise be...’** (36:48). On this assumption, the meaning would be: The messengers and the disbelievers all sought victory, and the disappointment was for the tyrants, which is the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **مِنْ وَرائِهِ جَهَنَّمُ وَيُسْقى مِنْ ماءٍ صَدِيدٍ** — **Beyond him is hell, and he shall be given to drink of a purulent water...** to the end of the two verses. *Sadid* (الصديد) is the pus flowing from a wound; it is an explanation for the water they are given to drink in Hell. *Tajarrru‘* (التجرع) is consuming the drink gulp by gulp continuously. *Isaghah* (الإساغة) is making the drink pass through the throat; it is said: *sagha al-sharab* (the drink passed easily) and *asaghtuhu ana* (I made it pass easily). Thus it is in *al-Majma‘*, and the rest is manifest.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ أَعْمالُهُمْ كَرَمادٍ اشْتَدَّتْ بِهِ الرِّيحُ فِي يَوْمٍ عاصِفٍ** — **The parable of those who defy their Lord: their deeds are like ashes over which the wind blows hard on a tempestuous day...** to the end of the verse. A "tempestuous day" (يوم عاصف) is one with a strong wind. It is a representation (تمثيل) of the deeds of the disbelievers in terms of the results that follow them, and an explanation that they are frustrated and void (حبط باطلة), having no effect in terms of happiness. It is like His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَدِمْنا إِلى ما عَمِلُوا مِنْ عَمَلٍ فَجَعَلْناهُ هَباءً مَنْثُورا** — **Then We shall attend to the works they have done and then render them as scattered dust** (24:23). Thus, their deeds are like particles of ash over which the wind blew hard on a day of strong wind, scattering it and leaving nothing of it. This is their parable in terms of their deeds.

From here, it appears that there is no need to imply anything in the speech and return it to something like our saying: "The parable of the deeds of those who disbelieved..." It is apparent that the verse is not from the completion of the speech of Moses, but rather it is like the result obtained from his narrated speech.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Mu‘awiyah ibn Wahb, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: "Whoever is given gratitude (الشكر) is given increase (الزيادة)—Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: **لَئِنْ شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ** (If you are grateful, I will surely enhance you)."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi al-Dunya and al-Bayhaqi in *Shu‘ab al-Iman* narrated from Abu Zuhayr Yahya ibn ‘Atarid ibn Mus‘ab, from his father, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "No one was given four and denied four: No one was given gratitude and denied increase, because Allah says: **لَئِنْ شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ**; and no one was given supplication (الدعاء) and denied response (الإجابة), because Allah says: **ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ** (Call Me, I will answer you); and no one was given seeking forgiveness (الاستغفار) and denied forgiveness (المغفرة), because Allah says: **اسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ إِنَّهُ كانَ غَفَّاراً** (Plead to your Lord for forgiveness, indeed He is all-forgiver); and no one was given repentance (التوبة) and denied acceptance (التقبل), because Allah says: **وَهُوَ الَّذِي يَقْبَلُ التَّوْبَةَ عَنْ عِبادِهِ** (It is He who accepts the repentance of His servants) (42:25)."

In the same source, Abu Nu‘aym narrated in *al-Hilyah* by way of Malik ibn Anas, from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad ibn ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn, that when Sufyan al-Thawri said to him: "I will not leave until you narrate to me," Ja‘far said: "Indeed, I shall narrate to you, but a multitude of narrations is not good for you, O Sufyan. When Allah bestows a favor upon you—and you love its remaining and continuity—then increase in praising (الحمد) and thanking (الشكر) for it—for Allah the Exalted said in His Book: **لَئِنْ شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ**. And when you find provision (الرزق) slow, then increase in seeking forgiveness—for Allah the Exalted said in His Book: **اسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ إِنَّهُ كانَ غَفَّاراً — يُرْسِلِ السَّماءَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِدْراراً وَيُمْدِدْكُمْ بِأَمْوالٍ وَبَنِينَ** —meaning in this world and the Hereafter (1)— **وَيَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ جَنَّاتٍ وَيَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ أَنْهاراً**.

O Sufyan, when a matter from a ruler or otherwise grieves you—then increase in saying: 'There is no power and no strength except by Allah' (لا حول ولا قوة إلا بالله)—for it is the key to relief and a treasure from the treasures of Paradise."

I say: Regarding this meaning, there are many narrations from the paths of both groups.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to ‘Umar ibn Yazid, he said: I heard Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) saying: "The gratitude for every favor, however great, is that you praise Allah."

In the same source, with its chain to Hammad ibn ‘Uthman, he said: Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) came out of the mosque and his mount was lost—so he said: "If Allah returns it to me, I shall surely thank Allah with the right of His thanking"—it was not long before it was brought to him, so he said: "Praise be to Allah" (الحمد لله). Someone said to him: "May I be your ransom, did you not say: 'I shall surely thank Allah with the right of His thanking'?" Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "Did you not hear me say: 'Praise be to Allah'?"

In the same source, with its chain to Abu Basir, he said: I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): "Does gratitude have a limit such that if the servant performs it, he is a grateful one?" He said: "Yes." I said: "What is it?" He said: "Praising Allah (الحمد لله) for every favor upon him in family and wealth; and if there is a right in what Allah favored him with in his wealth, he fulfills it. Among it is the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: **سُبْحانَ الَّذِي سَخَّرَ لَنا هذا وَما كُنَّا لَهُ مُقْرِنِينَ** (Immaculate is He who has disposed this for us, and we [by ourselves] were no match for it); and among it is His saying: **أَنْزِلْنِي مُنْزَلاً مُبارَكاً وَأَنْتَ خَيْرُ الْمُنْزِلِينَ** (Land me at a blessed landing-place, for You are the best of those who bring ashore); and His saying: **رَبِّ أَدْخِلْنِي مُدْخَلَ صِدْقٍ وَأَخْرِجْنِي مُخْرَجَ صِدْقٍ — وَاجْعَلْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ سُلْطاناً نَصِيراً** (My Lord! Admit me with a worthy entrance, and bring me out with a worthy departure, and make for me a helping authority from Yourself)."

In the *Tafsir* of al-‘Ayyashi, from Abu Wallad, he said: I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): "Do you see this favor manifest upon us from Allah—is it not that if we thank Him for it and praise Him, He increases us—as Allah said in His Book: **لَئِنْ شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ**?" He said: "Yes, whoever praises Allah for His favors and thanks Him—and knows that it is from Him and not from another—Allah increases His favors."

I say: The last two narrations interpret gratitude in the best way, and what we presented in the commentary—that gratitude is the manifestation of the favor in belief, speech, and action—corresponds to them. This is supported by the absolute nature of His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَمَّا بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ فَحَدِّثْ** — **and as for your Lord’s blessing, proclaim it** (93:11).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, he said: My father narrated to me, raising it to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): He said: "Whoever harms his neighbor out of greed for his dwelling, Allah makes him inherit his house." And that is His saying: **وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِرُسُلِهِمْ** —to His saying— **فَأَوْحى إِلَيْهِمْ رَبُّهُمْ لَنُهْلِكَنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ وَلَنُسْكِنَنَّكُمُ الْأَرْضَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ**.

In the two *Tafsirs*, *al-Majma‘* and *Ruh al-Ma‘ani*, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "Whoever harms his neighbor, Allah makes him inherit his house."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn al-Durays narrated from Abu Mijlaz, who said: A man said to ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib: "I am the most knowledgeable of people in genealogy." He said: "You do not know the genealogy of people." He said: "Indeed I do." ‘Ali said to him: "Have you seen His saying, the Exalted: **وَعاداً وَثَمُودَ وَأَصْحابَ الرَّسِّ وَقُرُوناً بَيْنَ ذلِكَ كَثِيراً** (and ‘Ad and Thamud, and the inhabitants of Rass, and many generations between them)?" He said: "I know the genealogy of that 'many'." He said: "Have you seen His saying: **أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَبَؤُا الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ — وَعادٍ وَثَمُودَ وَالَّذِينَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ لا يَعْلَمُهُمْ إِلَّا اللهُ** (Has there not come to you the account of those who were before you—the people of Noah, ‘Ad and Thamud, and those who were after them? None knows them except Allah)?" So he remained silent.

In *al-Majma‘*, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): "*Sadid* (الصديد) is the blood and pus from the private parts of the adulteresses in the Fire."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa’i, Ibn Abi al-Dunya in *Sifat al-Nar*, Abu Ya‘la, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, Abu Nu‘aym in *al-Hilyah* (who authenticated it), Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Ba‘th wa al-Nushur* narrated from Abu Umamah, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding His saying: **وَيُسْقى مِنْ ماءٍ صَدِيدٍ يَتَجَرَّعُهُ** (and he shall be given to drink of a purulent water, which he gulps). He said: "It is brought near to him and he finds it loathsome—when it draws near to him, it roasts his face and the scalp of his head falls off—when he drinks it, it cuts his intestines—until it comes out from his posterior." Allah the Exalted says: **وَسُقُوا ماءً حَمِيماً فَقَطَّعَ أَمْعاءَهُمْ** (and given to drink boiling water that cuts up their bowels) and He said: **وَإِنْ يَستَغِيثُوا يُغاثُوا بِماءٍ كَالْمُهْلِ يَشْوِي الْوُجُوهَ** (and if they plead for help, they will be helped with water like molten copper which scalds the faces).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding the verse, he said: He said: "It is brought near to him and he finds it loathsome—when it draws near to him, it roasts his face and the scalp of his head falls off—when he drinks, his intestines are cut—and torn under his feet—and there surely comes out from one of them like a valley of pus and purulence." The narration continues.

In the same source, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him): "The *‘anid* (العنيد) is the one who turns away from the truth."
